---
name: progress-md-phase-tracker
description: Create and maintain `PROGRESS.md` for phase-based implementation work. Use when executing a phase plan, before starting phase implementation, during long or multi-step work, after validation, when a phase is complete, or when future/out-of-scope tasks should be recorded for later phases.
---

# PROGRESS.md Phase Tracker

## Overview

Treat `PROGRESS.md` as a living phase checkpoint artifact. Create or update it before execution starts, refresh it during meaningful checkpoints, and finalize it only after checking the completed work against the phase plan.

## Before Execution

1. Read the active phase plan in `phase-plans/` and any referenced standards or concept docs.
2. Inspect existing `PROGRESS.md` if present. Preserve prior phase history and user-written notes.
3. Add or update an entry for the active phase before editing implementation files.
4. Record the planned execution shape, not just the final goal.

Use this minimum structure when creating a new file:

```markdown
# Progress

## <PHASE-ID> - <phase title>

Status: Planned | In progress | Validation blocked | Complete | Partial
Started: <YYYY-MM-DD>
Last updated: <YYYY-MM-DD>

Plan:
- <planned step or deliverable>

Current checkpoint:
- <what has been done so far or what is about to start>

Validation:
- <planned command/check> -> not run yet

Out-of-scope / future work:
- <future task, reason it is out of current phase, suggested phase if known>
```

Use the repository's existing `PROGRESS.md` style if one already exists.

## During Execution

Update `PROGRESS.md` at meaningful checkpoints:

- After planning is translated into concrete steps.
- After each major deliverable or subtask completes.
- Before switching from implementation to validation.
- After validation passes, fails, or is blocked.
- When discovering a scope mismatch, prerequisite gap, or environment blocker.
- Before final response if the user asked for frequent progress updates.

Keep entries factual. Do not mark a step complete until the relevant files, artifacts, or checks support it.

## Completion Update

When the whole phase appears complete:

1. Re-read the phase plan and compare it to the implementation.
2. Update `PROGRESS.md` with final status, completed deliverables, validation results, and any deviations.
3. If validation is blocked, mark the phase `Validation blocked` or `Partial`, not `Complete`.
4. Include exact commands run and short results.
5. Include generated evidence paths when the phase produces traces, scorecards, reviews, changelogs, or similar artifacts.

Do not claim phase completion in chat until `PROGRESS.md` reflects the same state.

## Out-of-Scope And Future Work

When work is useful but outside the current phase:

- Do not implement it unless the user expands scope.
- Add it under `Out-of-scope / future work`.
- State why it is outside the current phase.
- Link it to a future phase or category when obvious.
- Keep it separate from blockers. A future task should not make the current phase incomplete unless the phase plan requires it.

Example:

```markdown
Out-of-scope / future work:
- Add browser UI polish. Outside PHASE-04A because this phase only defines ASCII rendering; candidate for PHASE-08A.
- Add extra enemy archetypes. Outside current minimal dungeon scope; candidate for enemy variety phase.
```

## Edit Rules

- Preserve existing progress history.
- Prefer appending or updating the active phase section over rewriting the whole file.
- Keep timestamps concrete using the current local date.
- Separate planned, in-progress, complete, blocked, and future-work states.
- Avoid optimistic language that is not backed by files, tests, or artifacts.

# PHASE-14 — Item System: Triggers, Targeting, Identification, Curses

## 1. Objective
Items become playable: every trigger type fires its effect bundle through the interpreter, and the identification game works.

## 2. Context
GAME_DESIGN §7 (triggers, shapes, proc chances), §8 (categories, unidentified spawns, cursed gear ≤10% announcing on equip); UX §4 (known-vs-unknown contract).

## 3. Dependencies
13A, 13B.

## 4. Scope IN
- `src/engine/items/`: trigger dispatch — quaff, read, throw_hit (projectile path via geometry), equip_passive, on_hit/on_struck procs (chance via RNG substream), use-with-charges, step (interface for 18).
- Identification: per-run seeded appearance pools for draughts/notes/charms; identify-by-use; knowledge table on run state; weapon/armor bonus reveal on equip; cursed announce-on-equip.
- Per-category log/event lines honoring the "what is unknown" card contract.

## 5. Scope OUT
- Trap placement (18). Item generation/placement on floors (17/26). Economy pricing checks (Gate 1, PHASE-33).

## 6. Owned files
`src/engine/items/**`.

## 7. Task breakdown
| Task | Type | Objective | Owned files | Agent | Est / Timebox | Parallel with |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | implement | Trigger dispatch + proc chances + charges + tests | items/triggers.ts | Codex | 20m / 40m | — |
| 2 | implement | Identification system + appearance pools + curse reveal + tests | items/identify.ts | Codex (same session) | 15m / 30m | — |
| 3 | verify | End-to-end item scenarios on fixtures: unknown draught → quaff → identified across the run; thrown item hits along path; proc rates within band over 10k rolls | — (read-only) | Cursor | 15m / 20m | — |

## 8. Verification commands
`pnpm run check` · proc-rate statistical test · identification-persistence scenario test.

## 9. Completion criteria
1. Every trigger type fires correctly (test per trigger).
2. Appearance pools are per-run seeded and stable within a run (test).
3. Identification knowledge persists for the run and never leaks across runs (test).
4. Acceptance bar: a brand-new item defined as pure schema data is fully playable — quaffable, throwable, equippable — with zero new code.

## 10. Risks & escalation
This phase proves the whole "content is data" thesis; if any item category *needs* bespoke code, that's a design break — stop and report immediately.

# ENVIRONMENT.md — Living Environment Facts (STEP 0)

The single source of environment truth, pasted (or linked as mandatory reading) at
the top of **every** task brief as its STEP 0 block. Every fact a worker rediscovers
mid-task is a turn wasted; every lesson learned gets written here **the moment it is
learned** — by the orchestrator, on a worker's report (this file is locked to
workers).

Facts marked **[inherited]** come from previous-project rehearsals with the same
tools and must be re-verified by the Phase 01 environment spike; facts marked
**[verified]** have been confirmed in *this* repo, with the date.

## Codex CLI (`codex exec`) sandbox facts — codex-cli 0.137.0

- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** `.git` writes are **ALLOWED** under
  `--sandbox workspace-write` (empty commit succeeded) — the inherited "blocked"
  fact is REFUTED for this CLI version. Codex workers use AGENTS.md's
  direct-commit path under `Codex Agent <agent@codex.local>`; COMMIT_PLAN.md
  remains the fallback if a future version re-blocks.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Non-destructive `&&` chaining WORKS —
  inherited "chained commands blocked" REFUTED. `rm -rf` is rejected by policy
  before execution (CONFIRMED) — use Node `fs` removals.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** No browser available in-sandbox: no
  chromium binary; `npx --no-install playwright` fails. CONFIRMED — browser
  verification routes through the orchestrator/host.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Outbound HTTPS works with
  `-c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Host npm cache contains root-owned files →
  `npx` fails with EPERM in sandbox. Machine fix (human, optional):
  `sudo chown -R 501:20 ~/.npm`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Token usage: the `turn.completed` JSONL event carries
  `usage:{input_tokens, cached_input_tokens, output_tokens,
  reasoning_output_tokens}` — ledger total = input + output.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `codex exec` reads stdin when invoked without
  redirection — **always append `< /dev/null`** in scripted invocations; omitting
  it caused a 15-minute no-event stall (observed).
- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** Nested `codex exec` (Codex launched from
  inside a Codex sandbox) fails when `~/.codex` is read-only ("failed to
  initialize in-process app-server client"). `scripts/codex-run.sh` handles this:
  temp writable `CODEX_HOME` + `auth.json` copy, cleaned on exit. Note: auth.json
  is briefly copied to a temp dir — local-only, deleted on exit.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** `rm -f` against `/private/tmp` paths is
  policy-rejected in-sandbox; use Node/Python fs cleanup.
- **[inherited]** Shared ambient auth does not reliably tolerate concurrent
  `codex` sessions — **one Codex session at a time** (not re-tested; keep until
  a deliberate probe says otherwise).
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Stall rule works as written: no-event stall →
  relaunch identical brief once (with root cause fixed) → completed in ~7m.
- **[observed 2026-06-11, n=2]** Codex no-event stalls (2-event JSONL, then
  silence) occur intermittently even with stdin redirected; both recoveries
  succeeded on relaunch with `@brieffile` prompts. Prefer `@brieffile` over
  long inline prompts for codex-run.sh dispatches; keep the early-stall watch
  (static <500 bytes for 5m → alert) on every Codex dispatch.

## Cursor Agent CLI facts

- **[verified 2026-06-11]** On this machine, `agent` resolves to the **grok CLI**
  (`~/.grok/bin/agent`), NOT Cursor. The Cursor CLI is **`cursor-agent`**
  (`~/.local/bin/cursor-agent`, version 2026.05.24). All Cursor invocations must
  use `cursor-agent`.
- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `cursor-agent` has no `--trust` or `--workspace`
  flags; use `--print` (full tool access incl. write/shell), `--model <model>`,
  `--mode plan|ask` for read-only, `--output-format text|json|stream-json`, and
  set the working directory by `cd`-ing into the target worktree before invoking.
- Canonical invocation: `cd <worktree> && cursor-agent --print --model
  composer-2.5 "<bounded prompt>"`; read-only audits: `--mode=ask` with explicit
  no-edit instructions.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** Cursor workers CAN mutate `.git` (commit
  succeeded, reverted) — the AGENTS.md direct-commit path for Cursor is valid.
- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** `&&` chaining and outbound HTTPS both work
  in Cursor sessions; model identity `composer-2.5` confirmed; cwd = invocation dir.
- **[inherited]** macOS keychain errors on `agent status`/`agent models` → rerun
  with elevated access; record the occurrence here.
- Cursor sessions may run concurrently (separate worktrees); Cursor is the fan-out
  tool, Codex is the depth tool.

## Worktrees & repo facts

- **[observed 2026-06-11]** Parallel implement-workers on the SAME worktree:
  disjoint file ownership prevents edit conflicts, but `pnpm run check` runs
  collide with the sibling's half-written files. Either dispatch parallel
  implement tasks in separate worktrees, or instruct workers to gate only
  their owned test path (`pnpm test -- <owned dir>` + typecheck) and let the
  combined verification run the full gate after all siblings land.

- **[inherited]** `.env*` files are gitignored and do **not** propagate into new
  worktrees — the orchestrator copies them explicitly when (and only when) the
  brief requires a key.
- Worktree path convention: `../gg-wt/<phase>-<slug>`; branch `phase-XX/<slug>`.
- **[inherited]** Dev-server port collisions are common: read `${PORT:-3000}`,
  document an override; never hardcode a port.
- **[inherited]** A running/previously-run Next.js app pollutes typecheck via
  `.next/types/*` — clean `.next` before the typecheck gate.
- macOS shell: `date +%s` for timing (no `%N`); BSD userland (`sed -i ''` etc.).

## Gate commands (cheapest first — fail fast)

```
pnpm run typecheck   # tsc --noEmit (cheapest)
pnpm run lint        # eslint .
pnpm test            # vitest run (@live excluded unless CODEX_LIVE=1)
pnpm run check       # all three, in that order
```

**[verified 2026-06-11, phase 03]** — pnpm v10.28.2, TS 6.0.3 strict, vitest
4.1.8, eslint 10.4.1, zod 4.4.3. Dispatch workers via `scripts/codex-run.sh
<label> @<brieffile>` / `scripts/cursor-run.sh <label> <dir> "<prompt>"`.

## Update protocol

1. Worker hits an undocumented environment fact → states it in the final report
   under "environment discoveries."
2. Orchestrator adds it here (with date + [verified]) **before dispatching the
   next brief**. Encoding the lesson immediately is the habit that fixes most
   slowdowns — tribal knowledge is a defect.
3. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to
   [verified] / removes it.

The single live coordination file for this repository. The orchestrator (CLAUDE.md)
owns it; workers update only the rows and log entries their brief tells them to.
This file records *state*, never *design* — design lives in the doc spine
(NORTH_STAR, TECH_SPEC, UX, WORLD, GAME_DESIGN) and `phase-plans/`.

## Phase Rotation Procedure

When a phase completes (all checklist items ticked with evidence, human accepted):

1. Archive this file's phase sections to `docs/progress-archive/PHASE-XX.md`.
2. Reset: Active Phase, Task Queue, Phase Checklist; keep Validation Log's last 5
   entries; carry Backlog forward untouched.
3. Update Active Phase to the next phase plan; copy its completion criteria into
   the checklist.
4. Orchestrator commits the rotation as a single commit: `Phase XX: close & rotate`.

# PHASE-05 — Schemas & Content Vocabularies

## 1. Objective
The single source of truth for every content type: the closed vocabularies (statuses, effect verbs, behaviors, triggers, targeting) and the entity schemas composed from them, with protocol versioning.

## 2. Context
GAME_DESIGN §6 (statuses), §7 (effect verbs, composition rules), §9.2 (behaviors), §10 (quest objectives); TECH_SPEC §6 (Zod single source, provider schema landmines); NORTH_STAR §3 (freedom spectrum).

## 3. Dependencies
04B (bounds come from config), 04C. Serial — everything downstream imports this.

## 4. Scope IN
- `src/schemas/`: Zod schemas for: the 10 statuses, 16 effect verbs (with parameter bounds from config), triggers, targeting shapes, effect bundles (1–3 effects + trigger + shape), item definitions (all categories §8), enemy definitions (stats + 1–3 behaviors + 0–2 abilities + origin tag), NPC definitions (dialogue tree ≤3 deep, 2–5 choices, merchant inventory ≤6), quest definitions (6 objective types), trap definitions, narration beats.
- `PROTOCOL_VERSION` constant + stamping helper.
- Provider-compat constraints honored *now*: no root unions, all properties required (nullable placeholders), `additionalProperties: false` (TECH_SPEC §6) — so the manifest schema (PHASE-30) composes these without rework.
- Validation tests: valid fixtures pass; out-of-bounds and malformed fixtures fail cleanly, per schema.

## 5. Scope OUT
- No floor-manifest envelope (PHASE-30). No engine logic. No JSON-schema conversion (PHASE-29/30 proves it live).

## 6. Owned files
`src/schemas/**`.

## 7. Task breakdown
| Task | Type | Objective | Owned files | Agent | Est / Timebox | Parallel with |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | implement | Vocabulary schemas: statuses, verbs, triggers, shapes, bundles + tests | src/schemas/vocab/** | Codex | 20m / 40m | — |
| 2 | implement | Entity schemas: items, enemies, NPCs, quests, traps, narration + tests | src/schemas/entities/** | Codex (same session) | 20m / 40m | — |
| 3 | implement | Protocol version + stamping helper + test | src/schemas/protocol.ts | Cursor | 5m / 15m | after 1 |
| 4 | verify | Fixture audit: every vocab item from GAME_DESIGN present, every bound enforced, provider-compat rules obeyed | — (read-only) | Cursor | 15m / 20m | — |

## 8. Verification commands
`pnpm run check` · verifier's coverage table (GAME_DESIGN row ↔ schema) in report.

## 9. Completion criteria
1. All §6/§7/§9.2/§10 vocabulary entries exist as schemas with config-sourced bounds; nothing extra, nothing missing.
2. Malformed/out-of-bounds fixtures fail with clear errors (tested per entity type).
3. Provider-compat constraints structurally enforced (verifier checked).
4. Acceptance bar: PHASE-30 can build the manifest by composition only, and engine phases can import types without redefining anything.

## 10. Risks & escalation
This is the highest blast-radius phase: any shape doubt → stop and report (schema shapes are human-ask territory, CLAUDE.md §When Unsure). Do not "improve" GAME_DESIGN values in passing.

./runs/sessions/phase13b-oob-fix.jsonl:6:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' NORTH_STAR.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Generative Gaming — North Star\n\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\n\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Concept\n\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\n\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\n\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\n\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\n\n### Working fiction (revisable)\n\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \"the dungeon adapts to you\" inside\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\nfor the architecture.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\n\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\n\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\n\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\n  background while the current floor is being played.\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\n  freedom *cheap*.\n\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\n\n### Where the \"genuinely interesting\" lives\n\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\n   The Director's job description is \"be a great dungeon master,\" not \"be hard\" — surprise,\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \"Show me what the AI\n   invented for me and why\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \"dungeon diary\"\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\n\n---\n\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\n\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\n\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\n\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\n\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\n\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\nlanguage (the \"floor manifest\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\n\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\n\n### The freedom spectrum\n\n\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\nlives:\n\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\n|---|---|\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\n\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\n\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\n   maximum depth per run.\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\n   free-text gameplay commands.\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\n   identical actions = identical run.\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\n   the validation gauntlet.\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\n\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\n\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\n\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\n  trivially empty are rejected.\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\n\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\n\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\n\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\n  completionist, chaos gremlin) is fed to the Director.\n- Scored on: **validity rate** (gates 0–1), **solvability rate** (gate 2), **difficulty\n  band accuracy** (bot survival within target band), **novelty** (distance from content\n  bank and from the trace's previous floors), **responsiveness** (does the floor measurably\n  reference the trace it was given?), **latency and cost** per floor.\n- Thresholds are regression gates: a prompt \"improvement\" that drops solvability does not\n  merge.\n- Periodic agent-played full runs (an LLM player completing whole games) as an end-to-end\n  smoke of fun, not just function.\n\nA model or prompt change is treated exactly like a code change: benchmarked, compared,\naccepted on evidence.\n\n---\n\n## 6. The Player Experience (Demo Through-Line)\n\nThe five-minute experience the whole project optimizes for:\n\n1. Start a run. Floor 1 is gentle and watchful — the dungeon is learning you.\n2. Play a few floors. Notice the content bending: the items lean toward your style, an\n   enemy appears that counters your habit, narration nods at something you actually did.\n3. Meet an NPC who references your deeds and offers a quest that only makes sense for\n   *this* run.\n4. Hit the mid-run signature moment: the Director makes one bold, personal invention —\n   a named rival, a cursed gift, a floor built around your weakness.\n5. Win or die. Read the dungeon diary: what was invented for you, and what the dungeon\n   inferred about you to invent it.\n6. Start a second run. The dungeon remembers the first. The opening narration proves it.\n7. (For technical audiences) Open the artifact viewer: manifests, gates, rejected\n   generations, fallbacks — the machinery behind the magic, fully inspectable.\n\n---\n\n## 7. How We Build It: Agents Building the Agent Game\n\nThe project is also an exercise in agentic development. Two agent populations, two\ntimescales:\n\n- **Runtime agent** — the Director, inside the game, generating content for players.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Effect Vocabulary [HARD — the Director's verbs]\n\nThe atomic effects from which **all** items, enemy abilities, traps, and shrine-like\ninteractions are composed. ~16 verbs; each takes bounded parameters. This list is\nthe single most load-bearing table in the project: the engine implements exactly\nthese, and the Director's creativity is names + fiction + composition + parameters.\n\n| Verb | Parameters (bounds [HARD]) | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| `damage` | amount 1–12 | |\n| `heal` | amount 1–20 | |\n| `apply_status` | status (from §6), duration (per §6) | |\n| `cure_status` | one status or `all` | |\n| `buff_stat` | ATK or DEF, ±1–3, duration 5–20 | |\n| `nutrition` | +10–100 Fullness | |\n| `teleport_self` | random walkable cell | escape valve |\n| `teleport_target` | random walkable cell | |\n| `blink` | directed hop 2–4 tiles | |\n| `knockback` | push 1–3 tiles, collision = 1–3 dmg | |\n| `reveal` | map / items / enemies / traps (this floor) | |\n| `identify` | one carried item, or all of one category | |\n| `enchant` | weapon or armor, +1 (item cap +3) | |\n| `summon` | 1–3 entities **from this floor's roster**, adjacent cells | no novel entities mid-floor |\n| `transform` | re-roll target enemy into roster entity of ≤ equal budget cost | |\n| `dig` | remove wall tiles in a line, length 1–5 | |\n\n**Composition rules:** an item or ability bundles **1–3 effects** + one **trigger**\n+ one **targeting shape**.\n\n- Triggers: `quaff`, `read`, `throw_hit`, `equip_passive`, `on_hit` (weapon proc,\n  chance 10–30% [HARD]), `on_struck` (armor proc, same), `step` (trap), `use`\n  (tool, 1–5 charges).\n- Targeting shapes: `self`, `melee`, `bolt` (first target in line, range 3–8),\n  `burst` (radius 1–2, centered self or impact), `floor`.\n\nGrowing this vocabulary is the **intended** post-MVP expansion axis: add a verb to\nthe engine (with tests), bump the protocol version, and the Director's expressive\nspace multiplies. Verbs are added by humans via this file, never by the Director.\n\n## 8. Items and the Economy\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./runs/sessions/phase13b-spatial.jsonl:6:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' /Users/keigoshimada/.codex/skills/progress-md-phase-tracker/SKILL.md && pwd && rg --files -g 'ENVIRONMENT.md' -g 'NORTH_STAR.md' -g 'GAME_DESIGN.md' -g 'PROGRESS.md' -g 'phase-plans/PHASE-13B-EFFECTS-SPATIAL.md' -g 'src/engine/effects/*' -g 'src/engine/**/*.ts' | sort\"","aggregated_output":"---\nname: progress-md-phase-tracker\ndescription: Create and maintain `PROGRESS.md` for phase-based implementation work. Use when executing a phase plan, before starting phase implementation, during long or multi-step work, after validation, when a phase is complete, or when future/out-of-scope tasks should be recorded for later phases.\n---\n\n# PROGRESS.md Phase Tracker\n\n## Overview\n\nTreat `PROGRESS.md` as a living phase checkpoint artifact. Create or update it before execution starts, refresh it during meaningful checkpoints, and finalize it only after checking the completed work against the phase plan.\n\n## Before Execution\n\n1. Read the active phase plan in `phase-plans/` and any referenced standards or concept docs.\n2. Inspect existing `PROGRESS.md` if present. Preserve prior phase history and user-written notes.\n3. Add or update an entry for the active phase before editing implementation files.\n4. Record the planned execution shape, not just the final goal.\n\nUse this minimum structure when creating a new file:\n\n```markdown\n# Progress\n\n## <PHASE-ID> - <phase title>\n\nStatus: Planned | In progress | Validation blocked | Complete | Partial\nStarted: <YYYY-MM-DD>\nLast updated: <YYYY-MM-DD>\n\nPlan:\n- <planned step or deliverable>\n\nCurrent checkpoint:\n- <what has been done so far or what is about to start>\n\nValidation:\n- <planned command/check> -> not run yet\n\nOut-of-scope / future work:\n- <future task, reason it is out of current phase, suggested phase if known>\n```\n\nUse the repository's existing `PROGRESS.md` style if one already exists.\n\n## During Execution\n\nUpdate `PROGRESS.md` at meaningful checkpoints:\n\n- After planning is translated into concrete steps.\n- After each major deliverable or subtask completes.\n- Before switching from implementation to validation.\n- After validation passes, fails, or is blocked.\n- When discovering a scope mismatch, prerequisite gap, or environment blocker.\n- Before final response if the user asked for frequent progress updates.\n\nKeep entries factual. Do not mark a step complete until the relevant files, artifacts, or checks support it.\n\n## Completion Update\n\nWhen the whole phase appears complete:\n\n1. Re-read the phase plan and compare it to the implementation.\n2. Update `PROGRESS.md` with final status, completed deliverables, validation results, and any deviations.\n3. If validation is blocked, mark the phase `Validation blocked` or `Partial`, not `Complete`.\n4. Include exact commands run and short results.\n5. Include generated evidence paths when the phase produces traces, scorecards, reviews, changelogs, or similar artifacts.\n\nDo not claim phase completion in chat until `PROGRESS.md` reflects the same state.\n\n## Out-of-Scope And Future Work\n\nWhen work is useful but outside the current phase:\n\n- Do not implement it unless the user expands scope.\n- Add it under `Out-of-scope / future work`.\n- State why it is outside the current phase.\n- Link it to a future phase or category when obvious.\n- Keep it separate from blockers. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\n\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\n\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. The Concept\\n\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\n\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\n\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\n\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\n\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\n\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\" inside\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\nfor the architecture.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\n\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\n\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\n\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\n\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\n\\n### Where the \\\"genuinely interesting\\\" lives\\n\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\n   The Director's job description is \\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\" not \\\"be hard\\\" — surprise,\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\"Show me what the AI\\n   invented for me and why\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\"dungeon diary\\\"\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\n\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\n\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\n\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\n\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\n\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\nlanguage (the \\\"floor manifest\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\n\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\n\\n### The freedom spectrum\\n\\n\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\nlives:\\n\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\n|---|---|\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\n\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\n\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\n   maximum depth per run.\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\n\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\n\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\n\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\n\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\n\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\n\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"agent_message\",\"text\":\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\n\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\n\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. The Concept\\n\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\n\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\n\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\n\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\n\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\n\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\" inside\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\nfor the architecture.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\n\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\n\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\n\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\n\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\n\\n### Where the \\\"genuinely interesting\\\" lives\\n\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\n   The Director's job description is \\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\" not \\\"be hard\\\" — surprise,\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\"Show me what the AI\\n   invented for me and why\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\"dungeon diary\\\"\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\n\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\n\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\n\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\n\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\n\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\nlanguage (the \\\"floor manifest\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\n\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\n\\n### The freedom spectrum\\n\\n\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\nlives:\\n\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\n|---|---|\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\n\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\n\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\n   maximum depth per run.\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\n\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\n\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\n\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\n\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\n\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\n\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"agent_message\",\"text\":\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. I’m moving into the actual probes now and will record exact stdout/stderr for each.\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc 'git status --short'\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_10\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"rg --files -g 'package.json' -g 'pnpm-lock.yaml' -g 'package-lock.json' -g 'npm-shrinkwrap.json'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc 'git status --short'\",\"aggregated_output\":\" M ENVIRONMENT.md\\n M PROGRESS.md\\n?? runs/\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_10\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"rg --files -g 'package.json' -g 'pnpm-lock.yaml' -g 'package-lock.json' -g 'npm-shrinkwrap.json'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":1,\"status\":\"failed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_11\",\"type\":\"agent_message\",\"text\":\"The worktree is already dirty in files I’m not allowed to edit, and `runs/` already exists as untracked content. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.started\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":null,\\\"status\\\":\\\"in_progress\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\\\n\\\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\\\n\\\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\n\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\n\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\n\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\n\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\n\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_8\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation.
